content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

SUPERSEDED
by 18902.1(a)(4)

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 29, 1995


William M. Bonnell
Office of the City Attorney
City of Richmond
P.O. Box 4046
Richmond, CA 94804

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-240

Dear Mr. Bonnell:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
two members of the Richmond City Council under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTIONS

1. Can Mayor Rosemary Corbin, who owns a four-plex
residential rental unit, vote on the adoption of a proposed "Code
for Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling Units"?

2. Can Councilmember Donna Powers, who owns a single-family
home and rents it to a family member, vote on the adoption of a
proposed "Code for Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling
Units"?

3. Can both members participate in a vote to move the first
reading of the proposed code to a subsequent meeting?


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-95-240
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2.  Based upon the facts provided, the adoption of the
proposed "Code for Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling
Units" will not have a material financial effect on either council
member.  If this is the case, both councilmembers may participate
in this decision.

3.  Since it has been determined that the councilmembers will
not have a conflict of interest in decisions relating to the
ordinance, it is not necessary to address this question.

FACTS

The City of Richmond is considering an ordinance which would
create a new chapter in its municipal code, which would be known
as the "Code for Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling Units."
The ordinance would require the inspection of all rental dwelling
units and through such inspection, would identify and require the
correction of any violations of building and property maintenance
laws.  Such an ordinance would safeguard and preserve the stock of
decent, safe, and sanitary dwelling units within the city and
protect persons entering or residing in them by providing for a
regular and comprehensive system of inspection and repair.

Under the proposed ordinance, once a rental unit passes
inspection, a certificate of compliance would be issued and be
effective for a three-year period or upon change of property
ownership.

The current version of the ordinance defines "dwelling unit"
as "a residential dwelling unit in a single-family, two-family or
multi-family residential building, which is not owner-occupied,
including motels, hotels, rooming houses and boardinghouses and
similar living accommodations, but excluding mobile home parks."

The staff is considering an amendment to exclude from the
definition of "dwelling unit" buildings which are occupied by an
immediate family member.2/

The proposed ordinance seeks a $50 registration fee for
dwelling units.  The fee covers any other dwelling unit owned by
the same owner and located in the same building.  In addition,
there is an initial inspection fee of $10 for each dwelling unit,
whether located in the same or separate building.  Further, a re-
inspection charge, if required, of $25 is charged for each
dwelling unit, whether located in the same or separate building.

2/  In your letter you asked what affect this exclusion would have
on the councilmember whose single-family residence is occupied by
his son.  Since we have determined that the ordinance will not
have a material financial effect on the councilmembers, this
question does not need to be addressed.

Advice File No. I-95-240
Page 3


One member of the city council owns a four-plex residential
unit which is occupied by persons not related to the owner.
One member of the city council owns a single family home which is
currently occupied by a son.  The son pays an amount of rent equal
to the monthly house mortgage payment.

The staff intends to place the proposed ordinance, either the
current version or one excluding buildings which are occupied by
an immediate family member, back on an agenda-setting meeting for
council action.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

A public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family,3/ or on:

* * *

(b)  Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c)  Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regarding to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provisions to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(Section 87103(b)(c).)

The councilmembers each own residential rental property for
which they are also receiving income.  Accordingly, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the councilmembers' rental property or
sources of income will be materially affected by decisions
concerning the "Code of Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling
Units," the councilmembers must disqualify themselves from

3/  An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children.  (Section 82029.)

Advice File No. I-95-240
Page 4


participating in such decisions unless the decisions would effect
the "public generally" in the same manner as it would effect the
councilmembers' interests.

Reasonably Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     The proposed ordinance will impose fees for mandatory
inspections of rental property. Since the councilmembers both own
rental property, it is reasonably foreseeable that the ordinance
will have an effect on the councilmembers' interests. However,
before disqualification is required the reasonably foreseeable
effect on their property interests must also be material.

     The standard for materiality applicable to real property is
dependent on whether the real property is directly involved in a
decision or indirectly involved. (Regulation 18702.) If the
decision directly affects real property interests,
disqualification is required unless the decision will have no
financial effect on real property interests.

     An official's real property interest is directly involved if
the decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
government subdivision, of real property which the official has
a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more, or a similar
decision affecting such property. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(A).)

     The councilmembers' property is not the subject of zoning,
rezoning, annexation or inclusion or exclusion in any city
subdivision and is thus not directly affected by the ordinance.
However, an official's real property interests may be indirectly
materially affected and therefore require disqualification. The
indirect effect of a decision on real property interests is
material if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of:

     (A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on the
fair market value of the real property in which the
official has an interest; or

     (B) Will affect the rental value of the property
by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.

                              (Regulation 18702.3(c).)

Advice File No. I-95-240
Page 5


     In addition to the effects on the councilmembers' real
property interests, the councilmembers could not participate in
the decisions if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets or liability
increasing or decreasing by at least $250. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(4).)

     Assuming that each councilmember would pay the initial fees
for inspection, the fee assessed the owner of the four-plex would
be $90; $190 if re-inspection were required. The fee assessed the
owner of the single-family residence would be $60; $85 if re-
inspection were required.4/

     If the councilmembers were to pass this cost on to the
tenants of their respective rental properties, we would need to
consider the affect on the tenants of the rentals, as sources of
income, to the councilmembers. Regulation 18702.6 provides that a
decision is material as to a source of income who is an individual
indirectly affected by the decision if the decision will affect
the individual's liabilities by $1,000 or more.

     The fee imposed under the proposed ordinance would not meet
the thresholds for materiality under either Regulation 18702.3,
the indirect effect on real property, Regulation 18702.1(a)(4),
the effect on personal liabilities, or Regulation 18702.6 the
effect on an source of income who is an individual.

     Accordingly, it does not appear that Mayor Corbin or
Councilmember Powers would be prohibited from participating in
decisions concerning the proposed ordinance to establish a "Code
for Inspection of Residential Rental Dwelling Units."

     Since it has been determined that the councilmembers will not
have a conflict of interest in decisions relating to the
ordinance, it is not necessary to address the question concerning


_________________________

4/    This letter is based on the facts presented to us. This
advice would change if other economic interests not identified in
the letter would be affected by the ordinance. The Commission
does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Advice File No. I-95-240
Page 6


placement of the first reading of the ordinance on the council
agenda.

     I hope this answers your questions sufficiently.  If you need
further assistance, please feel free to contact the Commission's
Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Jeanette E. Turvill
                                             Political Reform Consultant
                                             Legal Division

SGC/JET/it
Enclosures
